speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Had some education but she got college down there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So then they all came over from Oregon?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, they started out in Missouri. She was born in Wisconsin but she was the only one, I think, the rest. Then they settled in Missouri and she came from... They settled in Oregon near Forest Grove. Of course, she went to college there. And then they heard about land being open here and they came here to settle. I don't know how they got up on this ridge over here.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Which is called potlatch?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah, big potlatch. I'm not sure I'm registering. It seems to be.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I had to check my dial to see if it was working there.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Is it working?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Yep, doing fine.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: About what year would this be?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: That was in 1880 when they came.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Here, anyway. And they were still land open for homestead. Yes, this was all open. They were just starting in to settle up there then. It hadn't been farmed. The Indians had hunted and so on here. But there hadn't ever been any farming done here. And about that time, I guess after it opened up, they could take out 160 acres. That's what you could homestead. But you had to live on it. Show that you were making improvements trying to do something about it. Well then, my father had came here from... They started out from Iowa. He was born in Missouri, but they lived in Iowa. My grandfather heard about the old strike in California. Well, he thought that was for him. He got there and he didn't find the gold. So he had 120 acres of farmland in Iowa. And he went to California and fooled around. They didn't make anything then. They come on up here and settled on 160 acres in homesteaded. Up on Overfix Ridge. Up on what they call the little potlatch. 13 miles. This side of Moscow. Oh, okay. And so then my grandmother, which I never had seen. She died before I was born. She had heard about some school teachers. It was over here. Heard about the Welkers, which was my mother's maiden name. So she took a horse to ride and wanted to lead. So she decided to go back with her to teach school. They were going to open up a school. So that's how she met my father. And he went to school with her. Only she was some older than he was. And she went to school to him and finally he talked to her and noticed her marrying her.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So he got the school arm, huh?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah, he got the school arm and the ranch with it. Oh, yes. You were telling me. Your grandparents homesteaded up here. Yeah, and an uncle of mine and my mother. They were the ones that were old enough. I think you had to be 21 before you got homesteaded. They were of course beyond the 21.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So they all three took homestead?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: They all joined. They were all right close together.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now was this unusual for a woman to take a homestead?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, I don't think necessarily. They were entitled to it. So I don't know. But I don't think there was one family up there we know of by the name of Hepler that the man and woman both homesteaded. But I know the others didn't kind of frown on that. They didn't think it was right. That's all I know about it. Of course I know the people. I remembered the people. But that was before my time.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, that homesteading is very interesting because of the improvements and things.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did your mother do her own work?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, she didn't. She done all of her own work. She only taught for a little while and then actually married. By that year she actually married, she had her first daughter. Well, then her first child. She never worked or taught after my mother. There wasn't such a thing as a doctor on that entire... Well, anywhere around here there wasn't a doctor. My mother had eight children. She never had a doctor for any of them. My grandmother had sent me out some sort of a book on childbirth. She was what they called a midwife. It was just what she read. My dad taught like that. It wasn't anything with her having those children. Because her mother was a midwife. Well, I'd like to see him have one or two of them anyway.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So your grandmother served as midwife?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah. She'd ride a horse. People knew of her then. People would hear about her. She had two older girls at home and said she could do the work at home. They'd see them coming and riding. They knew about that time that the woman was going, they'd go get her. She'd stay there and take care of the baby and do the work, cook her for them while she was away. So, of course, it would be such a long way to go a horseback. It would be way up on Cedar Creek for miles around. She'd ride and stay and take care of women when they had their babies.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was her name?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Welker. Mrs.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Welker?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah. That's the one my grandfather homesteaded. Of course, my father's name was Euler. He lived up on Little Potlatch. There was a Jess Euler that used to live near Moscow. He got the home place. But he was by the second wife. My grandfather had ten children with the first wife. Then she died. Then he married a woman much, much younger than him. He was in his sixties. He had four more children. My goodness. He was busy. But he was in the, he was, he was a soldier in the Civil War. I have a picture of him somewhere. Here of him in his uniform.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh, wow.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Then my father, he was never satisfied. He always wanted to sell to ranch. Well, it was really hers. She put off, put off until, she set a price of $40. That's where she made the mistake as quick as she got $40. She was a woman of her word and she sold it for that. Now, last year, I don't know if I've heard that they weren't so anxious to buy land right now as they were a year ago. If they didn't want to sell that same land, they could have got $1100 an acre for it. Sheesh.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Doesn't sound like much right now.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: They went through a terrible lot for them. Of course, $40 them times went quite a way. But they used to have a credit, you know, where these stores would get credit for a year. And my folks, they thought, they had about a $100 store bill and they thought they had an enormous store bill.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: They spend that for about a month now.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I couldn't have spent that much yesterday going down and trying to pledge them birthday presents for the little girls and for my daughters. Yeah.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: She died back in 1950, no, 1914. She died at the age of 57, but my father died in 1951. And he was 91 years old when he died. He didn't take things serious. He'd done more work with his mouth than he did with it otherwise. That didn't sound very good to some of my relatives.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But it's the truth, huh?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: He wasn't speaking of the truth. Ah, yeah. You were telling me that before about some of the times that you spoke up to your father.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I wanted to ask you while we were back and thinking about the homesteads and the Indians and things, you mentioned Mox Mox coming to dinner?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah, up on Little Potlatch Ridge. We were there one time, my aunts and uncles, they lived up there, had homestead. My aunt and uncle on dad's side, homestead, he was old enough, he homesteaded up on Little Potlatch. And he come there and ate dinner with them. They were friendly and he'd go there. But my sister, she wouldn't take any of the butter after he had taken some because when he got ready to put some butter on something, he took the knife and licked both sides of it and put it back in the butter. And then my sister, Ma, didn't want any more butter. Oh, he served himself and licked both sides of the knife. Yeah, he wanted a clean knife when he went to that. Made sure he got all the butter off.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have dinner there when he was eating there?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I suppose I did, but my sister is a year and a half older than I am and I don't even remember it, but she remembered it. But I never had to remember that part. I'll be darned. I remember their house how it was and all of that. They had taken a boy to raise, they hadn't adopted him. We had to help him get in the wood. He was an ordinary kid. Later they couldn't keep him. They let him go back and then he had to go to reform school. I think he's dead though now. They kept him there. There was a big high sidewalk where we had to go down the steps to get down to where the wood was. He pushed us off that big high sidewalk. When I started school that fall, I would have been six years old, but my sister was a year and a half old. We had to help, but then we had to get pushed off the sidewalk. I'll get it together. I mean, that's all I'm going to do.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have a lot of chores like that at home? Hauling wood and stuff like that?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Or was that... No, I worked more outside after I was married than I did after. When we went on and on. When I did at home, I wasn't... Well, I was married when I was 17. I was married. I had to work out. I made a living after I was 14. I worked at housework though. I tuitioned them and worked. That's all I knew is just to do housework. I worked for someone there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, that's interesting.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you live in the places where you worked?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yes, I had to live in them.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Of course, you aren't like them here where they treat their hired help. I know we always did anyway. People on the ranches here treat them the same as they would their family. But when you worked in town, those people served the meals and courses. You don't eat with them. You served at them and then you eat in the kitchen. And whenever they have a nice big front room and so on, but you go on up to your own room after that. But later on, my sister worked just across the street from where I worked. So we'd go to the show sometimes. About twice a week. That's about all week at Ford's when we were working. Five dollars a week while you did all of the housework and cooking. I was going to ironing for five dollars a week. Of course, five dollars went a lot farther than it does now.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But it was... I wish I could find someone for five dollars to do all the cooking. Housework.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: You do everything. And they didn't have electric washers then when I was working. Otherwise, they had one that... But I washed everything on the board. But the flat pieces like the sheets and the tablecloths. And the pillows they sent to the lobby. And the other things like that.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: They treat you pretty good outside of a...

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh yeah, but then... She was a lot nicer than her. She was a person who was born poor herself and then she married a man with a dad a little bit in the winter. The lady where my sister worked was a lot more thoughtful than what she was. There's just a lot of difference in people. Oh yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you work just in the one place?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, I worked at the other place. And listened to it. And I worked up here in Harvest. People that had thrashers. They used to thrash it all with the machine. Well, then they had to have help. And I'd hire out for that. But they wanted something I'd hire out.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did you do with the threshing? What did you do?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh, as a cook. Cook and peel potatoes and so on. I was just... Well, I was only 17 when I married. But my mother was sick and we had to learn how to cook and do things, do housework early. Because she was sick for a number of years. She had Brice disease, the kidneys, and a magnetic cure for that. But she never doctored and she always never complained. And they didn't know it. And my dad had went out over to harvest. He ran the thrash machine. The thrash machine...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was it?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: The thrashing tender of the machine. That runs that and thrashes the grain. And when he got home, why, she changed so much. He took her right away to doctor her. But it was too late. She only lived a month after that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was there a doctor here?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: There were doctors here, but then she just never complained. She'd have spells that she'd be working in the garden. And she'd get dizzy and pass out in the garden. And still she'd say, oh, it was just a bilge spell as well. What she called it. And she would never want to go to a doctor. So I can't really hardly remember her ever having a doctor. She didn't have her for her babies. She didn't have her for her babies. Boy, that's pretty good. I left though it by dad one time. He always was telling how much grit he had. And I knew he didn't have too much. So one time he was telling me about a doctor Mosher that pulled teeth, how he pulled this tooth. And he said how grit he was, nobody else but old John Weller. Because he always called himself old John. He could have stood that. So when I was about 13, I had to have a tooth pulled. And I went in there and Dr. Mosher told me all together a different story. Said he never hurt so much dust in his body.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I'm on right now. I'm on my way home. Your father's going to get you.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Your father's going to get you. I don't know if we happen to go the same way.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You were also talking last time about going to school. How many schools you went to. I started in Moscow.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: And I went there, I was in the fourth grade when I moved away. I was 10 years old when I was in the fourth grade. Well, he didn't wait until the school was out. They moved back over to Leland. That's where we started out in the farm in the first place. And moved over there. Every time he moved, he just made the wrong move. It cost him a little bit to move. And so we got over there. Finished up there. Then we went that fall. We started in Leland. And then a little later, he moved to Southwick and went there. So then the next year, I moved to Kendrick. We went back down to Kendrick. We started there before the school was out in the spring. We got back up in Cedar Creek. My sister had taken her eighth grade to get examinations. I was in the seventh grade. And she was through with that. But I went on up there to a crescent school. And I had to... That year, it was just a little bit of school left. So I didn't go anymore. But I started in and had my report card. They let me go ahead as far as that goes. But I had to stay until the term was out. So I started there. And I went that winter until in the spring before the school was out. When we were back in Leland. And I... That's where I finished up. I took my eighth grade examinations. And I ended up. And of course, that's as far as I ever got in school.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was there a big difference between the city school, like Moscow and... Yes, it was North Shore there.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Of course, up at Moscow, we not only had to write our spelling words down. We had to put the marks. It was along the ear like it was in the dictionaries or in your spelling book. And you'd get your written examinations. You had to know how to mark those. You'd know how to pronounce them. And then of course, after I got back over this way, I ended up in the fifth grade again. I'd never done that. I just learned to spell them. And that was all.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did the teachers have more time? Were the classes smaller?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, they just had one grade. And those up at Leland and the same at Southwick, they had more than one grade. And some of those schools, the country schools, they had all eight grades and all the subjects to teach. Well, they couldn't do just the spotted kids, you know, and teach that many. But these little country schools, they kind of dwindled down. People got so... The old folks got through having all those children, they didn't have so many anymore. When my son started school, there was a teacher there, was teaching all eight grades. She had 42 or 43 children that she had to teach and had all those grades. Well, when the girls went, they were eight years younger. Well, they were 17. So they dwindled down from that much in the time that the children started to go on summer school. He started the high school the year they started to grade school.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Yeah, I guess that would be right.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Yeah? Eight years?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: It didn't happen the next time I had children. I had one girl in about five minutes, I hadn't know where they were. They were all going together.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that a surprise? Did you know you were going to be a twin?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, I did not. Because I went down, when I got in the fall, when I felt sick, I went down to the doctor. He thought that's what it was, and I never did go back. Later on, I knew bless it good and well it was, but you didn't go and get checkups all by her. At least I didn't. On a Friday, I didn't feel good, and I sent and got Fred's sister. She rode horseback up to be with me, and he was way off back in the canyon somewhere, cut in the wood. Well, Willard was, he hadn't even started the school yet. Until that next fall. Oh yeah, he had to. He'd started the school. But he would have been in school in that day. Saturday was on. But I couldn't have sent him way back there anyway, because he wouldn't have known where to find his dad. So, he'd come home that night, and he thought, I had three weeks that I should go yet. He said, he bawled me out, sent for his sister for a minute, and then he said, if you want me again later, you're full and don't pay attention to Fred. So, I waited, went on, I was home all day, and on Saturday. And Sunday I got up, and I got his breakfast, and I got to God dang sick. And we had another barn where we had to stop down the lane from our place there. Another big barn down there. He had to stop, he fed them first that morning. Now he was getting pretty sick. And when he come to the house, he helped me fix the bed. And he started phoning to get some help, and boy, now he was getting excited. And the first thing he knew, he had two babies on his hands, and he didn't know what the world to do with them. No kidding. And Mrs. Stoneburner, there's a lady that came later, they picked her up and the doctor. When the doctor came, all he done was come in, look him over, and everything is alright and go back to town. But mud was so thick and so deep, and had such terrible roads that they had to, could just come to this little town three miles by our place with a car, and then they had to take a rig on. From there, Fred's brother brought the doctor on in, but we didn't need him. And Fred's brother -in -law got Mrs. Stoneburner, and she came, and she had the baby's old dress and everything, it was overtime, he got there, he just went to town.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So your husband is the one that delivered your twins then?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, he didn't have much to do about it. Well, he just asked me where this was at, and that was at.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did you do?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: But he was really excited.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, how about you?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: It wouldn't bother me at all. It would be something else it would have. I never have, I've been sick a lot. I never worry about myself. Just the other folks, the members of the family. I think I've come through some day, I'll look out, but anyway, like, my son, it was kind of funny with him. He likes to tease me. Fred did too, and if they thought anything makes you mad, they just keep saying that. So he got teased after his dad died about my boyfriend, after he lived here. He got into his boyfriend, so here a while back, I was out in Lewis, my daughter was sick, and I was down there sending the kids to school to take care of them, and he says, well, there's about three of those old guys that you chase around with their Giugetta sitting out on your porch, out there in their little porches when we came down. Well, I says, you look again when you go back tonight and tell them if they're still there to stick around, I'll be back.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You don't want to lose them, huh?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, he wanted to play the game, I played with him.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, you were saying your dad used to tease you too about marrying a German?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: A German, yeah.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: He'd get a little bit perturbed at me, because I talked back to him, Maude would talk back to him, so him and her got along fine, but I didn't like something out of him in Lewis. They usually know what I think. He liked Fred, that's my husband, he liked him alright, and he thought the world of his dad, he was a German alright, he talked about his dad there, Cagas Dutchman is what he called him. My sister went with a full -blooded German, the same as I did, and she married one.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Later on, of course, I married her, of course, she was, and I said, Well, I said, what's the difference between a Cagas Dutchman and a Crowside Dutchman?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Maude didn't like it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: She didn't like that at all.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was there a lot of rivalry between the German people?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh, there was later years, I don't know at first if there was, when they came here, but even Fred and I were married, and I started going to the church that he went to, the Cammin, the Lutheran church, they stuck pretty much to their German then, but you don't see that anymore. They had married in, but first, why, those older ones didn't want to marry anything but a German, and they didn't want them to talk anything but German around home, well, some of those old folks that had been here for years never did learn to speak English, and that's a whole lot of stubbornness than anything else, but they are an awful stubborn breed of people as far as I'm concerned. I lived them on for 44 years. You were an expert then. You're an expert then. Yeah, I know, they are, you're not going to tell them anything a lot, but they have, over in Germany, they don't have the resources you have here, and they have to do a lot of saving, and they came here, and these other people had come and homesteaded, well, they eventually got tired of it, and they'd sell out to the Germans, and the Germans, they'd build it all up and build nice homes and stuff, they'd come back and look at it after they sold, and saw how it turned out, but they didn't like it, but it was their own fault. Well, they could have done the same thing, but they just weren't industrious.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then the German people around here were especially industrious.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah, they are, and you'll notice up around Troy, too, that most all those people, the Swedes and Norwegians are pretty well fixed. They just don't have as much as they have here. They can't afford to waste it back there, they have to take care of everything. But my husband, he used the word hurry more than any other word there was ever in the dictionary, but he never told you to do anything but what he did and give you that hurry word with us. And that's just the way his folks lived, and they just go as hard as they could. And he expected everyone else to keep up.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Catch up with him if they could. Well, I hope my thing's working right, because my little dial isn't. That looks alright.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: There it goes.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Let's see, I had something else I wanted to ask you about.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: We've picked up some stories about a doctor in Kendrick.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What's his name?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I don't remember his name, but he was supposed to have been a real character. Oh, Dr.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Kelly?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Dr. Kelly. Dr.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Offel? Any of those?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: That doesn't ring a bell. Anyway, I wondered if you remembered any doctors.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I remember the ones back as far as... Then there was a Dr. Stormburner that was up at Leland. That was a little town there, up there two miles from where our homestead was. I don't know if I knew just what he might have done. Well, there was a doctor, and he was in Kendrick, and he was supposed to have had a special cure. And people would come all over to him. Must have been a witch doctor.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No, I've never heard of it. But I don't have the fella's name right now. I can't think of it. I don't know.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I may have heard of him. But I wouldn't know what in the heck he would have a cure for.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, I don't know if he was genuine anyway. I've never heard of that.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, I know Macy and I heard everything, and you tell her what she would tell you. She'll tell you one thing. She studies history. She does a lot of reading, and she can get history books. When Marsha comes in there, she always is there to take out books. And she likes to read. But she tells stuff that I happen to know different. Because it isn't anything to that. But she has told me that something is placed on the ward on this ridge that they farmed one time. I said, I've never been on that place. Don't give that old stuff to me. She says, you're a husband, and you come to our place. Many a time we live there. I've never been on that place yet to this day. I've been biased since that time. But I've never been on that place. I've never stepped foot on that place. The hill places. I've never been on that place. But she tells you something. There's two of them here. One's an old maid, and she's a widow. And they say something, and they're getting into the darndest arguments amongst them. I just shut up because there's no use talking because you're not going to be a minister. You don't want to take a side. Sometimes you'll say something before she gets through. Don't play that for her. No, no. I won't. She makes herself out of a liar. She contradicts herself. She's quite sound.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You were telling me last time about your dad.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: After your mother died, how he went out and how you didn't approve. No, he didn't. He'd get so mad. He tried to go with the women. There was a widow up there. Had been child. Little child. And she was in her probably upper 20s, somewhere along the mare. And he had kids, but he got as old as she was. He wanted to go with her. He just hated those younger guys. He wanted a quarter.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh, yeah. He did remarry. Later on, after mother died. At first he told us when mother was alive how no good she was. How wild she was. Thinking after he married her, then she tried to tell us how good she was. Unless he talked up to him, but I didn't. He was married to her. I just let him do his talking, but he didn't change my opinion. Anyway, he didn't have to know it. But I took care of him until three months before he died. And then I just couldn't anymore. I got so nervous. We took him and put him in the home and he'd done such terrible things. That's why I had to take him away. They were going to send him up to our phenol. He was 91. They had to give him a sanity hearing. They can't commit him there. It was right around the first year. It was a holiday. I knew good and well that there wasn't much we could do until after New Year's. But the third of January, Fred and I, my husband and I, went up and saw an alma vets up south. I told her just what little dirty stunts he'd done and so on. I just got to the point I couldn't take it anymore. So I said, well, she'd try. So we paid him. He was getting something. He never had anything when he died.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler

She liked to be on the end where you take but not to put anything out. So she's immersing home now down in Lewis. Of course, it'll be two years this spring that I was sick. I just kept getting worse. I would just feel it pass off and I'd pull her along until I put her in left foot too long. I had to also. I never had those awful pains in my life to get. But I'd get real pale and I'd put her to pass out. I would get dizzy. Maybe I'd get in the car and start somewhere and I'd get dizzy. I'd just put my car back in the shed. So finally the kids got hard boiled with me and said I had to go. Well I was getting where I couldn't take it much longer anyway. I got down there and of course they had x -ray and I stayed at my daughter's. X -ray. Come to find out I had gallstones. They had surgery but they had to get me ready. So they said about two more days that thing would have bursted. I didn't have just a lot of them like some of them do. But I just had one great big one and then some sand in there. But this one was enormous.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler

By that time I would start to read my paper and he would start to talk. So you missed out on a lot of things. Yeah I missed out on a lot of things. He was a talent. He liked to brag. Boy when you told one of his kids they were going to get a whip and they got it. And they just know neither of them are running. They just will come and get it. Take it because they were going to get it.

At that time you allowed horses and cows or something in town. I would go up there and hide in the hayloft too.

Or drag her under the sidewalk with me.

And the water supply and all those things. And then of course they had the outside houses and flies and all that sort of stuff. There used to be a lot of typhoid fever. I had a sister that had it. The one in the nursing home now. She had it when she was seventeen. And they used to have it. I have an uncle that died of it too. When he was about thirty. Close to forty. Oh my goodness. There must have been an awful lot of that going around there. And then I had this one boy. That one died of typhoid fever is what they called it. And the next one. He had a plane face. When he was born he had these like. Like oh there's a.

And of course she got real upset over that. And the baby's born. Well she blamed it to that but no one knows that. But he was born that way. And I think it was three weeks that he died. And the other baby was a year and a half old. He was just older than my sister that's the year and a half older than I am. And he had the group. And my mother was sick and she was in bed but the grandmother was there. But he choked to death in his sleep. Aww. So there was a. Right now there's three of us alive. Three girls. And my folks had four boys and four girls and they only raised one boy to be a girl.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: My older sister died of TB at that time and she had it. Well they didn't know any cure for TB. And all kinds of weather and possibly that it was that that caused that. She and her husband both died of that. Of TB before about three or four months apart. One died, she died in October and he died the following March. They left two little girls and then of course my mother took care of them as long as she lived. And my sister and I it was just older than me we had to take care of those little girls after that. And I'd work out a while and make some money to buy some clothes for myself. And then I'd go home and Maudie would go and work out so that she could just when I would stay home.

But my older sister she with Maud died she took care of us all the time and they just changed her past. She had two of them. I guess Maud wasn't broken a year and a half. I don't know I didn't have much luck with kids being fully broke until they were two years old.

I wouldn't know how many miles it is. I don't go that often. My granddaughter just told us one time she and I went to basketball. And we started down to come back home. We got there just fine. And they changed the roads up around Genesee somewhere. The road I intended to take I missed. So anyway, we found another one and I said, well this will take us down there. And I knew the houses and all that but they changed the road and put it around behind where some of those people were back there. And we were off of the main road. Well, then I got where I didn't know. And by gosh, we were lost if we got lost there. So there was a guy that used to live down here in Juliet and he works up there for a farmer. So he come along with a pickup and he said, I'll get you out on that main road and get you back on the road. Then I know you can find your way home, which we did. So when we started out, then my granddaughter started teasing me. He said, you told about your folks coming in covered wagons clear from back east.

If it ruffled her, you never knew it. If it bothered her. And I have a sister that's in nursing home now. She's just the other way. She gets all excited when someone would come in. But they'd come there. If they got any company, she didn't like to cook. They were still here. And they were poor too. They figured I had it all. It wasn't nothing for me because I had everything. I had that garden, but she didn't know how I worked out there to get that garden. And by gosh, if she knew that, they could eat cheaper. If it didn't take as much in gas to get them up there, that's by gosh where they come. And you could just rest assured, the day before the 4th of July, that's when we'd be cultivating beans or putting up hay. It would be one of the two. I'd have a crew. They'd come in right around noon on Saturday, the day before the 4th. And she wouldn't be ready. She'd have a bunch of iron. She never liked iron. And I guess the kids would be thrashing in through the doors and slamming along with my own. And she'd drag out the sewing machine. I'd done the cooking and I'd have to do the dishes. And then after Shadden got around to press her husband's suit, your boy's suit wasn't pressed. And so I was doing that. And I had my kids all lined out a month before the 4th. I knew I had to get ready. If I did, we wouldn't make it.

I was glad I was there. But they had an awful lot of work. Getting the little kids ready and everything. I don't know how I could do it. But I always managed to get through one way or the other.

words: That's why I was wondering, how many men did you feed?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well, at one time I was cooking for 47, I think. It was a big crew. It was just a big thrash machine. And then the separator tender had died. The guy had come, he'd never tended to separator before. He had a little bit of trouble to smash things up and I had him eight days that time. Oh, gee! But the worst of it was, he had the big ranges. But, they weren't to cook for that many, your stove wasn't big enough. But you could cook, they would hold the whole, I cooked. Baked plenty of loaves of bread a day. Ten in the morning, ten in the afternoon. I started out one meal, I made pies. And I had to have so much room in the oven that I couldn't do it. I could get the cakes out some way because it didn't take so long. I had to have twelve pies and I just didn't have time to get the meat in and the bread and all that stuff. I'd get up at three in the morning and I'd be still going at ten thirty at night. But I was glad when he was going. I was young then and strong, but I could take it now, I just couldn't do it. Eight days! Golly! Well, if you had a big enough stove, the stoves were larger, but you would have that room for that much stove in your kitchen.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: But now you take these stoves, now the electric stoves don't have as large ovens as the old ranges had.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No, they don't.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: But then, I couldn't get, I don't think I could feed forty -two out of my kitchen. You had to bake the bread.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did you serve them for breakfast? Just hotcakes?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, I only tried that about once or twice. I liked to work, that's too much. We'd fry potatoes and we'd have meat and eggs and cooked cereal.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: All that was easier than making pancakes?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh yes, because pancakes, you can't hardly flop them over fast enough. If I were fast enough to keep them going there on a, I set fourteen at a time so they could set, then I just have to, I had the twenty -seven, I had a bigger dining room there at that place, and I run two tables. But at the last, I just had this one big long table that's heated fourteen. They just had to take turns. And then they'd fill up the table and we'd have to get those dishes off right away and then set some more on there. But I only tried it once. I had hotcakes for breakfast and I had to do that very much work for that day. And then there was lunch.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you just make sandwiches?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Sandwiches and cookies usually. Sometimes cake. You had to take it, I had to take it, and then coffee. I had to take that to the field. At nine o 'clock in the morning in the afternoon, well, at four o 'clock for lunchtime. Then all the cups had to be washed and we got them back.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Two lunches?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Two lunches. One in the morning, one in the afternoon.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then they had a big dinner or a little dinner at noon?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Big dinner.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh boy.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And that's when you tried to get all the pies and everything.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: But then I...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you do this all by yourself?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, no, I had two helpers. I had a niece and my oldest niece, the one that's mine. And I had another girl, because we were just a baby then.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And you had all your own housework and trying?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: We didn't do a heck of a lot in the maker bags. They didn't need the baby. They had two of them, we would have been sweeping them all. You didn't do much rolling around.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And did they eat a big supper at night or just a little supper?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Yeah, heck yes. Big supper at night. I had a crew of 13 one time.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: In the beans. And those 13 ate as god dang much food as... They just happened to be a bunch where they all were hard eaters. And then they get to running up there where the guys would see which one could eat the most pie. And by god they carried it too far and we all, all those women roamed in there. We went together, we made up one. We put stuff for that. Heck, any of the family didn't get a piece of pie. They'd eat it right down the line. So we put it on by place so once a few extras eat, you get a piece of pie.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: And so how about you said something afterwards?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I said, well, you got to me to hold yourself. I said, that's just why you got limits.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was this taken out of their pay or was it just kind of included?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh, no, no, no. That was, the farmer fed the crew.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: They were food and they was boarded.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And they had to find a place for them to sleep?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: They had to bring the beds. They'd take hay or straw or something and fix it. No one could fix it. I've had the hay hands when they didn't have the beds and I had to fix the beds for them. But not the thrashers, they bring their own beds.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Just a bed roll. Just a bed roll.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I don't think they had invented this year's sleeping bags at that time.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I'm not. I think the bed roll worked just about as good. Boy, that sounds like so much work.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: It was. I wouldn't want to try it now, but I was on the day's work bed. I would like to try it now.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now what kind of crops did you have? You mentioned beans, right?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah, we had wheat and barley and oats and then the beans. So at those times, way back there, we didn't use it. Now they raise peas.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: The good ones are born. And then the next year was a wet year too. But we saved some, but then we lost a lot too. Then later on they had another wet year, a bad year, but we didn't happen to have any beans in that year. And we had quit. By that time we quit raising beans.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: But there's no one went up to it. From the time you start out in the spring to getting your ground ready to seed and plant the beans, then you've got to cultivate those beans and get rid of the weeds that's in there. And then when the beans is off, the hay is ready. And the hay was done and the harvest is ready. There wasn't any rest between. You were just one seed right after another.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now what did your father raise? Same thing?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, when he raised, when he farmed, well they had never started beans. I was about 10 or 11 years old the first time I ever remember them ever trying beans up on the ridge there and they had a flawed Craig raise some. It was just an experiment. And then he's done pretty good with it and then they everybody started again. They made bean cutters and so on and then they raised beans. These white navy beans and red beans, these dry beans.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And before that it was just oats and...

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oats and barley. And then they straight summer followed some. They'd just, about every, they'd rotate it to every third year. They'd summer follow a certain amount of it. They'd lay a third off or something like that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now what was this for weed control or?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yes, for them to build the land up.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: For the moisture?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Yeah, I suppose. Anyway, they would summer follow. They'd always have some. Now these farmers are there, they'd just straight summer and sell the vines if they had it the ones that are farming it now for the hind girders. But they're good farmers.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: My son started out and he had more meat to take care of so then my hind girders, I put in that and the paper and got people to run me a ragged here. I put in a ragged here, a safter brood down here for a durettit.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then some guys showed up wanting to rent it and I said, do you have tractors and all this stuff?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well he's got a tractor, I said you've got to have one tractor to farm. Well I, they could go out and try to rent a ranch and they didn't have any more idea what they was up against than anything. They'd be all this kind of, they'd have to have their living for a year until they got that crop off. They'd have to have money enough to buy the seed because I wasn't about to buy their seed and take a chance and someone didn't know anything about farming. In fact I wouldn't rent someone that didn't understand farming. Well after I had that, well I figure these guys get through, I don't think I'll run another, ever run another ad, well I'll ask a few farmers that I think kind of half ways know that they want to farm and are good farmers. I don't want to be bothered. Yeah. Because I've got some up there that I know who would be about the first to put them down and I like them and I get along with them. But everything has to be their way and I'm sure that now we're getting along but if we, I rented to them we wouldn't so I find it, there's going to be, I know what I had to talk about. I'm not going to indulge in anything like that. You'd be surprised guys, dumb heads that go out and think they could farm and they never farm in their life.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Yeah, that's a nice dream. Yeah, they had to feel good while they were hunting.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: When did the horses go out around here?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh they started quite a number of years back at getting, having tractors. But Fred, my husband, he was the last one I think on that old ridge that's still farmed to the horses and didn't have a tractor. And he didn't get a tractor until the, well it was in the spring of the, Willard was in the service in World War II and he had worked in the mines when he left here and he decided to go back and try that. And when he got out and got home and said, he was born in 44, about 46 or something when he got out of the service and got home. And he farmed in that next fall and he started farming in 47 but that was when we had, that spring my husband had bought a tractor and he was a stopping farmer and of course the boy took the tractor then and then later they got a crawler but this was the job.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He used horses all the way up in the 40's.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: When we were first married he had 30 workcourses that he used.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Hmm, I see what you mean he had to go feed the stock before he came in for breakfast.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He had a lot to feed, huh?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: And he'd raise, he'd raise all his horses. He'd have those bears and raise colts and he'd break them when they were three years old and he'd break those horses to work.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that long before you were born?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh yeah, they were just little fellas at first.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Hmm, did you ever take any of your animals up to the stock shows in Genesee?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: No, we never went in for anything like that. We didn't even have a truck for a long, long time. Hmm, no we didn't have anything like that. In fact I think he and I went to two, two fairs I don't think at all of the time. He had got crippled up and he didn't like it. He could walk with him. Now I don't care to go because I can't, even the shop, shop now I was shopping yesterday, the kids give them things. That walk had his, yup, he got arthritis in the knees. As long as I sit down they don't hurt, but boy. See my leg is all twisted out of shape here. See here this is all built up with that calcium or whatever it is. Yeah, the semen doesn't help that at all. It makes me look like I'm bow -legged.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: This doctor, Hewlett I think is his name, a chiropractor in ..., he says, have you always been bow-legged?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I said no, it's just since I had that arthritis. Well he insisted I had to go to him, all he did do wasn't do me any good, of course he x -rayed and give me a treatment. And to come back two days and take another night doesn't happen. They paid him his $60 he wanted and I ain't going back anymore because I, he doesn't do it, it doesn't help. He told me that day that he didn't think he could help me.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: If it ain't going to help I didn't want to go just to, he's got about all these things that live for himself.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did they do for arthritis back in the old days? If your dad had arthritis what would he have done?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I don't know, never had it.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: He was just a spry, only he got old and got weak and he could walk, he didn't have a limp. There's a man down here that's 82 years old, lives down here and by God he don't limp.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: And I says how come?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I says you don't limp and all these others, the rest of us have to walk with a cane, cane and all that. He says well I'll have to remember to limp but I don't buy it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Just for show huh?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well can't blame it all on hard work because you had your share while you were cooking and stuff.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Well I don't know what, I don't know what. Sometimes from being the knowledge peddler he had it, that doctor says jumping down off of the machinery and exposure to the cold and stuff. That's all I know, what he told him. I wouldn't have done anything about it if it had been Willard. Willard insisted but Willard's my son, he's got arthritis too and he's crippled up, he's 56 and he's crippled up. My sister's got it, she's 26 so you can't win these days. I've got it all through here but I know what goes that. I went there and we had a fence that was leaning down so I had to crawl up under like this and go over the top and it was, I was pretty darn heavy and I wasn't getting very good, doing very good if I'd have struggled a little bit. I'd have made it alright. And I tried to go through the barn and through the gangland we had a door where you could walk through and you had an elevator and I was supposed to go out there and help you with a liver session but husband died to help you load some hay and go with him to feed out around the canyon. And about that time one of those bales decided he didn't want to stick on that, letting them come down the elevator and it fell off of there and hit me right smack dab on top of the head. Just off me down and I couldn't get up, I just felt like the back of the road, oh it did hurt. So Willard happened to look out and look down there and heard that funny noise. My breath's coming funny, every time I breathe it's just like it's killing me. So he looked down there and he come down on that elevator and he come down there to go get me up and I said don't move me, don't move me. I didn't want him to move me, it just felt like it killed me. I went there to get up on my own, sit there for a little while but I didn't go and help him so then I went to the house and I decided well maybe I better go to the doctor anyway so I called Mary, my daughter -in -law. And asked her if she could come down and she said who is it and she didn't even recognize my voice because I was. And so I told her and she came and left a note for Judy. I called the school to tell her I was going to like scare her to death. She finished her senior year with me after my husband died. And it felt like it scared her to death. So I went down there to the doctor and I took treatments from a guy down there, not chiropractor by the name of Warren, Warren somebody.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I got me back in shape, I went a little bit.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I got a bump there in the middle of my back. But this here one of those twins was in a plane crash and her back was broke in four different places and it was all splintered. There wasn't anything they could ever set it or anything. And she was so bad and her lung had collapsed, one lung had collapsed. She laid out from 3 in the afternoon on the Sunday till the next night. Time to get her down off this mountain. That plane crash was dark when they got her off the mountain. It couldn't have been where it had killed him. It was in a certain place but he walked out to get help. So then he told them where it was at and they went back up there and they couldn't find it. They didn't go far enough up the mountain. And so then he had to go back up as far as they could go with a jeep. They took him that far and they started him up a draw and he started him out. So then they went back and they found her. Her feet were frozen partially and she lay there. And one guy died and there were four of them in the plane. The four passed the plane. He helped get her out of the plane and collapsed after that and died. That's right. She told me that. And they took her out there to Dr. Tanner was his name and he laid down. We didn't find out about it until Monday night about 8 o 'clock, 7 or 8 o 'clock. And Judy got word. And then she came down rather than call me and they had friends here playing cards. And I said, gosh, I want to go but I said I haven't got only $6. I said my purse. The Schillerly ones, they were the most wonderful neighbors. He says I've got $100 you can have. And so I wrote him out a check for $100. And he had $150 though when he counted it out. He said you can have it all if you want. And I said no. So I gave him his check. Of course he gave me money the next day. I'm sure they were the most wonderful neighbors I ever saw. He moved away now but he died. This is an awful friendly town. I'll have to say that much. It makes you bite to stretch the truth once in a while. But anyway, they are. They are an awful friendly town. Well you lived out of town most all your life then.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What? You lived out of town most of your life?

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: I spent five years in a hospital when I was a kid. I went there and went to school. I was 10 when I left. I thought well maybe I'm an 8 of you. If I were just going at 6 I can't remember what year it was you moved to a hospital. But I know that's where I started the school when I was in the 10th. I remember I was 10 years old when I moved away from there. The rest of the time I outfitted one litter was spending a kinder than me. My dad worked at the warehouse and we went to school while we lived on the farm. That's really the only work I know. That and cleaning house and stuff. The look of mine now you'd never think I ever did clean. It looks fine. Oh my god my windows. I got storm windows there and they should come off. And it takes two husky guys to get it off and I ain't even one husky guy.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: But it does save a lot of heat I think. It does yeah. I never had that in my head that that would help to make a house cooler in the summer. But by gosh it tells it on the TV and on the news or on the TV and in papers that that keeps your house cooler to add double windows. But I never figured it that way.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I think there's a sun coming in the window that's supposed to heat up your room. And you've got that second piece of glass and the heat gets trapped in between before it gets all the way in. When it gets against the windows I just I draw my drapes in.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: Oh yeah. I never leave a couch sitting in front of my room. But anything where the glass reflects it.

speaker: Ada Oylear Schoeffler
words: What do you do in your children? Do you have babysitters for them?
